summary: After twenty-plus years on Mac, DHH describes his journey away from Apple, first to Windows (which he found surprisingly capable thanks to WSL), then to Linux on a Framework laptop. He discovered that Windows is a legitimate alternative to Mac for web developers, but ultimately fell in love with running Ubuntu despite its rough edges. He found the tinkering and troubleshooting inherent to Linux nostalgic and genuinely enjoyable rather than frustrating, comparing it to the IKEA effect. He concludes that we now have three viable personal computing platforms and encourages adventurous users to try the switch.
key_points: Windows has become a genuinely good web development platform thanks to WSL, and most anti-Windows sentiment is based on outdated facts
Linux's rough edges and need for tinkering are features, not bugs — the hands-on experience creates a deeper connection with the hardware
The Framework laptop's hardware and AMD chip running Linux outperformed expectations and became his daily driver
Having three viable computing platforms (Mac, Windows, Linux) is healthy for the industry and consumers
Apple's missteps provided the motivation needed to overcome decades of platform inertia
money_quotes: It felt nice. Nice knowing that there was a real, realistic, and compelling alternative to the mac, and that most of my aversion to Windows was based on outdated facts or misconceptions.
Most of the jokes about Linux are true! There are more rabbit holes, more gotchas, and less polish.
Rather than being frustrated with things not just working out of the box, I embraced the adventure.
Computers required some assembly, and as IKEA knows, it made us love them more.
It's hard to know whether you'll find some zen of motorcycle maintenance in knowing how to tighten the timing chain of Ubuntu before you try.
We ought to have AT LEAST three good options for personal computing in the modern age, and now I've come to realize that we do.
Do it not because it is easy, but because it is hard. See what kind of computing stuff you're made of.
key_insight: Breaking free from Apple's ecosystem revealed that both Windows and Linux have matured into legitimate alternatives, and the hands-on tinkering required by Linux can be a source of joy rather than frustration.
